---
title: "Colorectal Cancer Atlas Re-Analysis: Harmony vs scVI Integration Benchmark"
subtitle: "Single-cell RNA-seq of MMRd and MMRp colorectal tumors (Pelka et al., 2021)"
author: "Marko Zivanovic, PhD"
date: "2026-05-25"
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    fontsize: 10pt
    colorlinks: true
    fig-pos: H
    pdf-engine: pdflatex
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---

# Summary

We re-analyzed the publicly available colorectal cancer (CRC) single-cell atlas from
Pelka et al. (Cell, 2021) to benchmark two widely used batch-integration strategies
on a balanced cohort of mismatch-repair-deficient (MMRd) and mismatch-repair-proficient
(MMRp) tumors. Eight donors (4 MMRd + 4 MMRp), tumor samples only, were selected from
the CELLxGENE-curated atlas, yielding 56,022 cells after QC. We applied **Harmony**
(linear PCA-based correction) and **scVI** (deep generative model on raw counts) using
donor as the batch covariate, and quantified both batch removal and biological signal
preservation. The two methods occupy distinct points on the integration trade-off curve:
Harmony delivered the strongest batch mixing (donor mixing entropy 1.82 vs 1.04 baseline,
+75%), while scVI gave the cleanest lineage structure (silhouette 0.293 vs 0.198 baseline,
+48%). Compositional analysis recovered the canonical MMRd immune signature: elevated
T/NK/ILC infiltration in MMRd tumors versus plasma-cell dominance in MMRp tumors,
consistent with the immunotherapy-responsive phenotype of microsatellite-unstable CRC.

# Dataset and cohort selection

The source data is the CELLxGENE-hosted release of the Pelka 2021 CRC atlas
(collection ID `4d82bd8e-8827-410b-8fc7-685b7dd92585`,
DOI 10.1016/j.cell.2021.08.003). The full atlas contains 370,115 cells across 62
donors and 7 lineage categories. We restricted analysis to tumor-derived cells
(`disease = colon adenocarcinoma`) and selected the top four donors by cell count
within each MMR status group, balancing the comparison and keeping runtime tractable
on a workstation-class CPU.

**Selected donors:**

- MMRd (MSI-like): C123, C130, C139, C170
- MMRp (MSS-like): C124, C129, C162, C171

After per-cell QC (n_genes >= 500, pct_mt <= 25) and gene filtering (min_cells >= 10),
the working object contained **56,022 cells x 23,415 genes**. Donor-aware highly
variable gene selection (Scanpy `seurat_v3` flavor on raw counts) retained 3,000
genes for downstream embedding.

# Methods

**Normalization.** Counts were normalized per cell to 10,000 and log1p-transformed.
The raw count matrix was retained in a separate layer for scVI training.

**Baseline embedding.** PCA on scaled HVG expression (50 components) followed by
nearest-neighbor graph (k = 15, 30 PCs) and UMAP.

**Harmony.** harmonypy v0.0.10+ called directly on the baseline PCA. Default theta
and lambda; 20 maximum iterations; convergence reached at iteration 5.

**scVI.** Variational autoencoder trained on raw counts with `donor_id` as the batch
covariate. Architecture: 10-dimensional latent space, 2 hidden layers, 128 units per
layer, negative binomial likelihood. Training: up to 50 epochs, batch size 512,
learning rate 1e-3, early stopping with patience 8, CPU backend.

**Clustering.** Leiden (igraph backend, resolution 0.6) on the integrated neighbor
graph for each method.

**Integration metrics.** Two complementary scores were computed on 5,000 randomly
sampled cells (seed fixed):

- *Donor mixing entropy*: Shannon entropy (base 2) of donor labels among the k = 50
  nearest neighbors of each cell in the integrated embedding. Higher = donors are
  more uniformly mixed locally = stronger batch correction.
- *Lineage silhouette*: silhouette coefficient against the author-provided lineage
  labels (`ClusterTop`). Higher = lineage clusters are more compact and well-separated
  = biological signal preserved.

# Results

## Integration quality (Figure 1, 2)

| Method      | Donor mixing entropy | Lineage silhouette |
|-------------|----------------------|--------------------|
| Uncorrected | 1.044 +/- 0.959      | 0.198              |
| Harmony     | **1.821** +/- 0.814  | 0.255              |
| scVI        | 1.671 +/- 0.774      | **0.293**          |

Harmony achieved the strongest donor mixing (+75% over the uncorrected baseline) and
moderate lineage preservation (+29%). scVI traded a small amount of batch mixing
(-8% relative to Harmony) for the cleanest lineage geometry (+48% over baseline,
+15% over Harmony). The trade-off is consistent with the published benchmarks of
Luecken et al. (Nat Methods, 2022): linear methods tend to over-mix while preserving
less fine-grained biology, while deep generative models give more compact lineage
clusters at the cost of slightly weaker batch removal.

![Harmony vs scVI integration. Top: Harmony embedding colored by donor (left) and
by author-provided lineage labels (right). Bottom: scVI embedding, same colorings.
Both methods recover the 7 major lineages; differences are visible in donor mixing
fidelity and lineage compactness.](../figures/hero_harmony_vs_scvi.png){#fig-hero width=100%}

![Integration trade-off: mixing entropy vs lineage silhouette. The uncorrected
baseline sits in the low-low corner; Harmony moves furthest along the x-axis,
scVI furthest along the y-axis.](../figures/integration_tradeoff.png){#fig-tradeoff width=70%}

## Lineage composition: MMRd vs MMRp (Figure 3)

Aggregating across all eight donors, the lineage composition recapitulates the
canonical immunological signature of microsatellite-instable CRC:

- **T/NK/ILC fraction is roughly twice as high in MMRd tumors** (~0.32 vs ~0.16).
  This is consistent with the elevated neoantigen burden of MMRd tumors driving
  cytotoxic lymphocyte infiltration, and is the mechanistic basis for the
  FDA-approved use of immune checkpoint blockade in MSI-high CRC.
- **Plasma-cell content is markedly higher in MMRp tumors** (~0.26 vs ~0.03).
- Epithelial and myeloid fractions are similar between the two groups.

![Per-MMR-status lineage composition. MMRd tumors are dominated by T/NK/ILC and
epithelial compartments; MMRp tumors carry a substantial plasma-cell
fraction.](../figures/mmr_lineage_composition.png){#fig-mmr width=85%}

## Donor-level heterogeneity (Figure 4)

The per-donor heatmap exposes a known caveat: aggregate compositions hide within-group
variance. Donor C171 (MMRp) is an outlier with > 0.45 plasma-cell fraction, single-handedly
driving most of the MMRp plasma signal. Donor C170 (MMRd) skews myeloid-rich, and C123
is the most T/NK-dominated MMRd case. Any downstream claim about MMR-associated cell-state
shifts at the patient level should be conditioned on this heterogeneity; an N = 4
comparison is not a statistical assertion of differential abundance.

![Per-donor lineage fractions. Red row annotation = MMRd, blue = MMRp. Note the
plasma-rich C171 outlier and within-group spread.](../figures/mmr_donor_heatmap.png){#fig-heatmap width=85%}

# Discussion

This re-analysis serves three purposes:

1. **Reproducibility.** The lineage structure described by the original authors is
   recovered with off-the-shelf Scanpy pipelines and 3,000 HVGs, without manual
   curation. The `ClusterTop` author labels achieve a lineage silhouette of 0.29
   in scVI latent space, indicating that the modern integration toolchain agrees
   with the manual annotation.
2. **Method-choice guidance.** For a downstream task that requires aggressive
   removal of donor-level effects (e.g., joint clustering across many patients
   to find shared cell states), Harmony is the practical default. For tasks
   requiring well-separated cluster geometry (e.g., reference building, label
   transfer, or scArches-style projection), scVI's latent space is the better
   substrate. The numerical gap between them is small in absolute terms but
   consistent in direction.
3. **Biological signal.** The MMRd-elevated T/NK/ILC fraction is internally
   consistent with the known immunology of microsatellite-unstable colorectal
   cancer and provides a sanity check that the integration has not erased
   biological covariates correlated with batch.

# Limitations

- N = 4 per MMR group is too small to support statistical claims about MMR-associated
  cell-type frequencies; the descriptive comparison should be read as a sanity check,
  not a discovery.
- The mixing-entropy and silhouette metrics are sampled (5,000 cells each); they
  are sufficient for ranking but not for fine-grained calibration. Full benchmarks
  (kBET, LISI, ASW per lineage) would refine the picture.
- scVI training was capped at 50 epochs on CPU. Longer training, particularly with
  a GPU, could marginally improve the integration metrics.

# Reproducibility

All code, conda environment specification, and Docker recipe live in the project
repository. The CELLxGENE dataset is downloaded directly from the public mirror;
no manual data acquisition is required.

- Source: <https://github.com/zivanovicmkg/scrnaseq-portfolio>
- Project subdirectory: `05_colorectal_atlas/`
- Environment: scanpy 1.11.5, harmonypy >= 0.0.10, scVI 1.4.2, PyTorch 2.7.1 (CPU)
- Total runtime on a 16-core / 62 GB workstation: ~45 minutes.

# References

- Pelka K, et al. *Spatially organized multicellular immune hubs in human colorectal
  cancer.* Cell 184, 4734-4752 (2021). DOI: 10.1016/j.cell.2021.08.003
- Korsunsky I, et al. *Fast, sensitive and accurate integration of single-cell data
  with Harmony.* Nat Methods 16, 1289-1296 (2019).
- Lopez R, et al. *Deep generative modeling for single-cell transcriptomics.*
  Nat Methods 15, 1053-1058 (2018).
- Luecken MD, et al. *Benchmarking atlas-level data integration in single-cell
  genomics.* Nat Methods 19, 41-50 (2022).
- Wolf FA, Angerer P, Theis FJ. *SCANPY: large-scale single-cell gene expression
  data analysis.* Genome Biol 19, 15 (2018).
